title: A moody trip into the dark depths of a dungeon - Crypts of Obscurum (review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=pyuB406DLuo

Daniel (@dungeondive) reviews Crypts of Obscurum, a gamebook by Daniel Cortez (physical and PDF on DriveThruRPG) that he pegs as a mix of old Fighting Fantasy, Escape the Dark Castle and D100 Dungeon. Instead of drawing a map you lay out playing cards, each corresponding to a numbered entry; you descend four dungeon floors of nine rooms plus an Ace-card boss, making choices, rolling tests, and managing hit points and sanity. He praises the grimy, well-written flavour and notes the first playthrough is the magic — discovery is the draw, so he keeps spoilers light.

His favourite mechanism is the dice-assignment system: you slot a d12, d8, d6 and d4 across your four stats (guts, skill, insight, resolve), then resolve everything as die-versus-die tests. Three classes with four backgrounds each, keyword-driven weapons, a spellbook with push-your-luck pandemonium and misfire tables, sanity checks that can teleport you to the final room, and randomly-generated Diablo-style legendary weapons all add texture. He role-plays his Templar through several atmospheric rooms — bone halls, gnashing undead, a forge that wants witch balm. A characterful, replayable solo gamebook he recommends (ideally the PDF).

----

Crypts of Obscurum's standout is assigning your d12/d8/d6/d4 across four stats, then resolving die-vs-die. Do you like that kind of upfront dice-allocation puzzle, or do you prefer fixed stats and straight rolls?
